seed: I keep overthinking everything at work, and I'm scared I'm going to get fired even though nobody said anything.

transcript.content: I keep overthinking everything at work, and I'm scared I'm going to get fired even though nobody said anything.

It sounds like you're feeling quite anxious about your job, and this anxiety might be causing you to overthink things. Can you help me understand why you think you might get fired? What specific actions or thoughts are leading to this fear?

Yeah, I guess I do feel anxious about it. It’s just... I keep replaying conversations in my head, wondering if I said the wrong thing or if I missed something important. Sometimes it feels like I’m not meeting expectations, and I can’t shake this feeling that any small misstep could lead to being let go. It's exhausting, really.

It sounds like you have high standards for yourself and fear making mistakes, which might be causing feelings of anxiety and nervousness. Have you ever considered that sometimes, perfection isn't possible or even necessary? Can you think of a time when a small mistake didn't lead to negative consequences as you initially feared? This could help you see that your expectations may not always align with reality.

I appreciate that, but it’s hard to let go of that feeling, you know? I often think back to times when I messed up, and it feels like those moments define me. There was that presentation at work a few months ago... I stumbled on my words and I felt like the whole room was judging me. I know it’s not the end of the world, but it still makes

It sounds like you've been carrying a heavy burden of self-criticism after your presentation at work. You might be thinking that those moments define you, and that's causing you some distress. Can we explore together why you believe that making a mistake in front of others means you are defined by it? And what would happen if instead, you saw these moments as opportunities for growth rather than defining events?

Yeah, it does feel heavy. I guess I’ve always struggled with feeling like I have to be perfect to be accepted or respected. It’s like, if I mess up even a little, it proves all my fears right. I don’t know, the idea of seeing those moments as growth feels... almost impossible sometimes. What if I just mess up again? What if I can’t bounce back

It sounds like you're carrying a heavy burden of self-expectation. You mentioned that fear of making mistakes and the thought that you must be perfect to be accepted or respected is quite overwhelming. Let me ask, have you ever considered that everyone makes mistakes and it's part of the human experience? Could there be a chance that your mistakes are not defining moments but opportunities for growth and learning?

I hear what you’re saying, and I know, logically, that mistakes happen to everyone. But when I’m in the moment, it feels like it’s just me who can’t get it right. I feel so much pressure to keep it together, and I worry about what others think. The idea that mistakes could be about growth? I want to believe it, but it's hard to shake that

It sounds like you have high expectations for yourself, and when things don't go as planned, you may feel like a failure. This can lead to feelings of anxiety and pressure. However, it's important to remember that everyone makes mistakes, and they can indeed be opportunities for growth.

Can you think about a time when you made a mistake, but managed to learn something valuable from the experience? This might help shift your perspective and make it easier to see mistakes as stepping stones rather than setbacks.

I guess there was that time I messed up a project deadline. I felt awful at first, like I had let everyone down, but I did manage to figure out how to communicate better with my team after that. It’s just... I still replay the embarrassment in my mind, you know? Even if I learned something, I can’t shake that initial feeling of failure. It’s like those moments

It sounds like you've been carrying a heavy burden of self-criticism since the project deadline incident. You seem to believe that your mistake was unforgivable and that it has tarnished your reputation with your team. However, have you considered that everyone makes mistakes? Could it be possible that your team understands this and is more focused on your efforts to improve rather than the initial error?

Let's explore this idea together. Can you think of a time when someone else made a mistake in your team, but you still respected them for their honesty and willingness to learn from it? This might help us understand if your assumptions about yourself are fair or if they

Yeah, I guess I can think of a couple of times when teammates messed up and still earned my respect. Like that one colleague who completely miscalculated their numbers on a report, but they owned up to it and worked overtime to fix it. I admired their honesty and how they asked for help. But when it comes to my own mistakes… I don’t know. It's like I feel like

"It seems you value honesty and hard work, especially when others admit their mistakes. Yet, you find it challenging to own up to your own errors. Can you help me understand why you might perceive your mistakes differently from those of your teammates?" (Socratic Questioning)

It’s true, I really do value those things in others. But when it’s me, I just... I feel this wave of shame that washes over me. I think part of it is that I always compare myself to others, and I feel like I should be better, like I should know more. I guess I see my mistakes as proof that I’m not as competent, while I can

It sounds like you have high standards for yourself and often find yourself comparing yourself to others. This comparison might lead you to believe that your mistakes are a reflection of your competence, causing feelings of shame.

Have you ever considered what would happen if you shifted this perspective? What if your mistakes were seen as opportunities to learn rather than proof of incompetence? Let's explore this idea together. When you make a mistake, what could be one thing you might learn from it?

It’s just hard for me to see it that way. In those moments, I feel this wave of shame wash over me. Like, I know I should be better, and I look at others and think they have it all together. I see my mistakes as proof that I’m not as competent. It's frustrating.

You seem to hold the belief that you should always perform optimally and compare yourself unfavorably to others when things don't go as planned. This thought pattern might be causing feelings of shame, frustration, and a lack of pride.

Let's explore this together. Can you share an example of a situation where you felt this way? And what evidence can you find that supports or contradicts the idea that you should always be perfect and that others are flawless?

Yeah, I see what you mean. There was that meeting last week where I felt like I was the only one who didn't have all the answers. I stumbled over my words, and I just kept thinking everyone was judging me. But then I remembered a few times when other colleagues didn’t have the right info either, and they didn’t seem to suffer for it the way I felt I did.

It sounds like you're feeling anxious about not having all the answers during that meeting, and you've been thinking everyone was judging you. However, have you ever noticed that others might also struggle with finding the right information at times? This could help shift your perspective a bit. Can you think of any instances where colleagues didn't know something and still managed to contribute effectively?

I mean, yeah, I can think of a few. Like that one guy who always asks questions during meetings, even if he doesn’t have the full picture. He just dives in, and it seems like people respect him for it. And I guess he doesn’t seem to dwell on his mistakes like I do. But when it's me, I just... I can't help but feel like I'm falling
